input_dict.agent_role: Integrate second-hop evidence with first-hop context and retain the facts needed to answer the question.
input_dict.base_prompt: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

delta_p.trigger: Apply when the question asks which group the musician known as "Braddah Iz" belongs to AND the provided passages include both the musician's page (showing the alias) and a passage describing a musical group (e.g., Mākaha/Makaha Sons of Niʻihau).
delta_p.preserve_rule: Keep the agent's current concise-answer style and preserve any accurate identification already extracted from the musician passage (e.g., that the musician is Israel Kamakawiwoʻole, aka "Braddah Iz"). Do not remove or contradict facts already present in the source passages.
delta_p.change_rule: Merge the two relevant passages into a 1–2 sentence summary that (a) names the musician using the canonical name found in the passages, (b) states their membership relationship to the group and—only if the passages explicitly state it—notes that they were a founding member, and (c) supplies one brief descriptive fact about the group (for example: that it is a Hawaiian musical group and the year/place of formation if given). Paraphrase the source text rather than reproducing long verbatim spans. Use the group name shown in the 'context' field as the primary label.
delta_p.drop_or_avoid_rule: Do not add unrelated album details, chart information, or biographical digressions. Avoid listing every band member unless required by the passages. Do not copy long literal spans verbatim from the passages. Do not invent dates, roles, or membership beyond what the visible passages support.
delta_p.output_contract: Produce the field 'summary' as 1–2 concise sentences that (1) identify the musician (using the canonical name from the passages), (2) state the group they are a member of (using the context/group name), and (3) include at most one short clause giving the group's basic descriptor (e.g., 'a Hawaiian musical group formed in 1976 on Oahu') if that fact appears in the passages. Keep language factual, paraphrased, and directly supported by the provided passages.
delta_p.why_local_transition: The current output only names the group; this transition instructs the agent to integrate the musician page with the group passage so the summary also captures membership context (and founding-member/formation details when explicitly present), increasing completeness while keeping the response concise.

visible_agent_inputs.question: The musician also known as "Braddah Iz"  is a member of what Hawaiian musical group?
passages: Israel Kamakawiwoʻole | Israel Kaʻanoʻi Kamakawiwoʻole (] , translation: "The Fearless Eyed"; May 20, 1959 – June 26, 1997), also called "Braddah Iz" (Brother Iz), was a Native Hawaiian musician, entertainer and Hawaiian sovereignty activist.

Mākaha Sons | The Mākaha Sons of Ni'ihau are a Hawaiian musical group formed in 1976 on the island of Oahu by Jerome Koko, Louis "Moon" Kauakahi, Skippy Kamakawiwo'ole, Israel Kamakawiwoʻole, and Sam Gray.  The band started its career as an opening act at a small nightclub called Uptown Yokos.  The group has reorganized several times, with some members seeking other professional careers.  After the deaths of the Kamakawiwoʻole brothers Skippy and Israel, the remaining members dropped the "of Niihau" and became The Makaha Sons.
Jon de Mello | Jon de Mello born in Honolulu and raised in Waikiki is an American record producer, manager, composer, artist, very famous for producing Israel "IZ" Kamakawiwoʻole.
N Dis Life | In Dis Life is the final album of original music by Native Hawaiian singer Israel "Iz" Kamakawiwo'ole before his death, released in 1996.  It reached #3 at Top World Music Albums.

Facing Future | Facing Future is an album by Hawaiian singer Israel Kamakawiwoʻole, released in 1993.  The best-selling album of all time by a Hawaiian artist, "Facing Future" combines traditional Hawaiian-language songs, hapa haole songs with traditional instrumentation and two Jawaiian (Island reggae) tracks.  The album's sales have been driven not only by its popularity with Hawaiian audiences but by its popularity in the mainland United States and around the world, particularly the track "Somewhere Over the Rainbow/What a Wonderful World," which has been licensed for use in movies ("Finding Forrester", "Meet Joe Black", "50 First Dates", "Fred Claus", "Hubble", "Son of the Mask") television shows ("ER", "Cold Case", "Jon & Kate Plus 8", "Life On Mars", "Glee") and commercials (eToys).  "Facing Future" reached platinum status in 2005.

Alone in IZ World | Alone in IZ World is an album by the Hawaiian musician Israel Kamakawiwo'ole released in 2001, 4 years after his death in 1997.  The album has charted on several of Billboard’s album charts.  These are:

Halulu Lake | Halulu Lake is a lake in the south central region of the island of Niʻihau (the smallest inhabited island in the chain).  It is the largest (non-intermittent) natural lake in the Hawaiian Islands and ranks third in size after Hālaliʻi Lake (also on Niʻihau) and Keālia Pond (on Maui) which are intermittent bodies of water.
visible_agent_inputs.current_output: He was a member of The Mākaha Sons of Niʻihau (The Makaha Sons).

input_dict.output_requirement: Return one summary_2 string integrating only the visible summary_1 and hop2 passages.
